steps.text: Load and unload the rotors and centrifuge safety cups in the BSC or another containment device.
Routine decontaminate laboratory equipment after spills, splashes, or other potential contamination, and before repair, maintenance, or removal from the laboratory.
Decontaminate equipment or material that might be damaged by high temperatures or steam using an effective and verified method, such as a gaseous or vapor method.
Ensure a method for decontaminating all laboratory waste is available in the facility, preferably within the laboratory.
Consider decontamination of the entire laboratory when there has been gross contamination of the space, significant changes in laboratory usage, major renovations, or maintenance shutdowns.
Verify decontamination processes on a routine basis.
Wear protective clothing with a solid-front, such as tie-back or wrap-around gowns, scrub suits, or coveralls.
Decontaminate reusable clothing before laundering and change clothing when contaminated.

Use eye protection and face protection (e.g., safety glasses, goggles, mask, face shield or other splash guard) for manipulations or activities that may result in splashes or sprays of infectious or other hazardous materials.
Dispose of eye protection and face protection with other contaminated laboratory waste or decontaminate after use.
Wear two pairs of gloves when appropriate.
Consider respiratory protection and ensure staff wearing respiratory protection are enrolled in a properly constituted respiratory protection program.
Consider shoe covers.
Assess the risk of mucous membrane exposure and wear protective equipment (e.g., face shield, surgical mask, goggles), as appropriate.
Consider additional PPE for persons working with large animals.
Consider respiratory protection for staff enrolled in a properly constituted respiratory protection program, based on the pathogen and work performed.
Ensure ABSL-2 facilities are separated from general traffic patterns of the building and restricted, as appropriate.
Ensure external facility doors are self-closing and self-locking.
Restrict access to the animal facility.
Ensure doors to areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed open inward, are self-closing, are kept closed when experimental animals are present, and are never propped open.
Provide a handwashing sink at the exit of the areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed or manipulated.
Ensure emergency eyewash and shower are readily available, easily accessible, and appropriately maintained.
Maintain sink traps filled with water and/or appropriate disinfectant to prevent the migration of vermin and gases.
Design, construct, and maintain the animal facility to facilitate cleaning and housekeeping, ensuring interior surfaces are water-resistant.

constraints.text: Protective clothing is not worn outside of the laboratory.
Additional PPE may be required based on work being performed.
Wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) when in contact with NHPs.
Ensure access to the animal facility is restricted.
Doors must remain closed when experimental animals are present.
Ensure traps are filled with water or disinfectant to prevent vermin migration.
Construct animal facilities to be water-resistant for housekeeping purposes.
Use personal protective equipment and ensure handwashing to decrease the risk of laboratory-acquired infections.
For activities with a high potential for aerosol production, a BSC must be used and centrifugation should be performed using autoclavable, aerosol-tight rotors and safety cups.
Importation of this agent requires CDC and/or USDA importation permits.
Domestic transport of this agent may require a permit from USDA APHIS VS.
A DoC permit may be required for the export of this agent to another country.
PPE should be used to cover exposed skin when working with cultures or fecal specimens that may contain infective hookworm or Strongyloides spp. larvae.
Special care is warranted to ensure that contaminated surfaces and equipment are thoroughly cleaned.
Precautions are warranted even when working with formalin-fixed stool specimens.
Ascarid eggs can be deactivated by the use of heat at or above 60ºC for more than 15 minutes.
All macaques regardless of their origin should be considered potentially infected.
Macaques should be handled with strict barrier precaution protocols.
The correct use of gloves, masks, and protective coats, gowns, aprons, or overalls is recommended for all personnel while working with non-human primates.
Some form of barrier is required to prevent droplet splashes to eyes, mouth, and nasal passages.
Specifications of protective equipment must be balanced with the work to be performed.
Caution should be exercised when handling specimens that could potentially contain SARS or MERS-CoV.
